cards.front: Name the 5 essential characteristics of cloud computing per NIST.
cards.back: (1) On-demand self-service. (2) Broad network access. (3) Resource pooling (multi-tenant). (4) Rapid elasticity. (5) Measured service (pay-per-use). NIST SP 800-145.

IaaS (Infrastructure): you get VMs, storage, networking; you manage OS and apps (AWS EC2, Azure VMs). PaaS (Platform): you get runtime/database/middleware; deploy code, ignore OS (Heroku, App Engine). SaaS (Software): finished application; you just use it (Office 365, Salesforce). Increasing managed-by-provider scope.

cards.front: Compare public, private, hybrid, and community cloud deployment models.
cards.back: Public: shared infrastructure, multi-tenant (AWS, Azure, GCP). Private: dedicated to one org, on-prem or hosted. Hybrid: mix of public and private with orchestration/data exchange. Community: shared by orgs with similar needs (gov, healthcare).

cards.front: Why does cloud computing affect WAN design?
cards.back: App traffic shifts from data center → cloud (often outside the corporate WAN). Internet bandwidth at branches becomes critical (formerly all traffic backhauled to HQ). Drives SD-WAN with direct internet access (DIA) and cloud onramps (Cisco Umbrella, Zscaler).

cards.front: What is a virtual machine vs a container at a high level?
cards.back: VM: full guest OS + virtual hardware via hypervisor (VMware, Hyper-V, KVM). Heavy, slow boot. Container: shared host kernel, isolated user space (Docker, containerd). Lightweight, sub-second start. Containers usually managed by orchestration (Kubernetes).

cards.front: What is a hypervisor and what's the type 1 vs type 2 distinction?
cards.back: Software that runs and manages VMs. Type 1 (bare-metal): runs directly on hardware (ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM, Xen) — used in data centers. Type 2 (hosted): runs on top of an existing OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation) — used on developer laptops.
